name: 3D & Advanced Animations
shortDescription: Real-time interactive 3D rendering with React Three Fiber, a particle system, and scroll-triggered animations.
description: Interactive real-time 3D rendering demo: an orbitable scene with PBR materials, a full solar system with a camera that chases the focused planet, an 8,000-particle galaxy with a hand-written GLSL shader, and a scroll-driven journey through the author's own tech stack.
longDescription: A responsive 3D scene, with realistic lighting, PBR materials, and an orbitable camera can be the difference between a plain page and one that grabs people's attention toward your product or system. The interactive solar system is a demonstration of what's possible with 3D on a modern website. The particle system with a custom shader shows the use of WebGL and 3D math. Even scrolling can become engaging through a GSAP-like approach, camera control with drei, and more.
tags: Three.js, React-Three-Fiber, WEBGL, 3D, Shader, GLSL, Scroll, Animation
architecture: Interactive scene with OrbitControls, Camera that chases the focused target, Particle System with GLSL Shader, Interactive Scroll
concepts: 3D Scenes, Solid Materials, GLSL Shaders, Particle System, Scroll with Camera, 3D Performance
commonSceneIngredientsTitle: The three ingredients of any 3D scene
commonCameraDesc: Defines the observer's point of view — like a film camera. Move the camera and the whole perspective changes. Without a camera, the scene isn't visible.
commonLightDesc: Without light, 3D objects are invisible — just like in real life. The light's position, color, and intensity define how every surface looks.
commonMeshDesc: The visible object. Every mesh has two components: Geometry (the shape — cube, sphere, text) and Material (the look — color, shininess, roughness).
commonInteractionTitle: How to interact with the scene
commonScrollDesc: Scroll or pinch to zoom in or out
commonMobileDesc: Touch and drag to orbit — pinch with two fingers to zoom
orbitIntroDesc1: Traditional websites are two-dimensional: elements have width and height, but no depth. With WebGL — a technology native to modern browsers — it's possible to render complete three-dimensional spaces directly in the browser, with no plugins and no installs. The computer's GPU calculates perspective, shadow, and reflection in real time, 60 times a second.
orbitIntroDesc2: This demo uses React Three Fiber, a library that integrates the Three.js 3D engine with React — letting you describe 3D scenes with the same component syntax used to build regular interfaces.
orbitElementLogoDesc: RoundedBox geometry with the '</>' symbol rendered on the four visible faces. The material is metallic with a real reflection — metalness 0.3, roughness 0.2.
orbitElementTextDesc: "Dev Showcase" and "Premium Portfolio" rendered as real 3D geometry, with bevel and depth. It's not a CSS font — it's a mesh of triangles calculated from the font's outlines.
orbitElementFloatDesc: The whole group gently floats up and down, forever, without a single line of manual animation code — using the Float helper from the drei library.
orbitElementEnvDesc: Lighting and reflections coming from a real 360° sunset panorama. That's what gives it the photorealistic look — the material reflects the surrounding environment like a curved mirror.
orbitElementShadowDesc: Simulates the sun lighting the scene from above. The shadow is a "soft shadow" — with a diffuse, realistic edge, not the hard, pixelated outline of basic shadows.
orbitElementPbrDesc: PBR = Physically Based Rendering. The materials physically simulate how light interacts with metal and rough surfaces — the same algorithms used in AAA games and animated films.
orbitDragDesc: Click and drag to orbit the camera around the objects
galaxyIntroDesc1: So far, the objects in the other demos were ready-made Three.js shapes (spheres, rings). Here, each of the galaxy's 8,000 particles has its position, color, and brightness calculated by a shader — a small program written in GLSL that runs directly on the GPU, thousands of times per frame, 60 times a second.
galaxyIntroDesc2: It's the technique used in games and award-winning sites to create visual effects that don't exist as a ready-made object in any library — just math running on the graphics card.
galaxyElementSpiralDesc: Each particle is positioned with polar coordinates (angle + distance from the center), creating the spiral arms — it's not a random cloud.
galaxyElementShaderDesc: The shape, color, and brightness of every particle are calculated by a program that runs directly on the GPU (vertex + fragment shader), not by a pre-made texture.
galaxyElementColorLabel: Color by distance from the center
galaxyElementColorDesc: Particles near the center blend toward orange/red; particles at the edges blend toward blue/purple — color interpolation calculated per particle.
galaxyElementRotationDesc: Particles farther from the center rotate more slowly than the ones near it, just like a real spiral galaxy.
galaxyElementGlowLabel: Glow with no image at all
galaxyElementGlowDesc: The round glow effect on each particle is pure math in the fragment shader (gl_PointCoord) — no texture was ever loaded.
galaxyElementExplodeDesc: The button launches the 8,000 particles to random positions and then pulls them back into the galaxy shape, interpolating in real time.
galaxyMouseDesc: The galaxy tilts smoothly, following the cursor's position
galaxyExplodeButtonDesc: Launches the particles outward and then pulls them back in
solarIntroDesc1: This demo is a whole solar system rendered in 3D right in the browser. The Sun, all 8 planets, and the Moon orbit in real time, with size, distance, and speed proportional to real values (compressed to fit the screen).
solarIntroDesc2: Technically, this runs on WebGL — a feature native to modern browsers that uses the computer's GPU to calculate perspective, light, and movement 60 times a second, with no plugins needed. The code uses React Three Fiber, a library that integrates the Three.js 3D engine with React, letting you describe a 3D scene with the same component syntax used to build the rest of the site.
solarElementToonDesc: Instead of a smooth, realistic lighting effect, the planets shade in a few discrete steps — the same visual style used in cartoon games, with no image required.
solarElementOutlineDesc: Every planet has a second, slightly larger sphere facing inward, which creates that dark border around it — the classic technique used to give it a drawn look.
solarElementTextureDesc: Earth's continents and Jupiter's bands aren't photos — they're drawn on the fly by the browser, mathematically, with no image ever loaded.
solarElementScaleDesc: The Sun, 8 planets, and the Moon orbiting with size, distance, and speed proportional to real values (compressed to fit the screen).
solarElementCameraChaseDesc: On click, the camera animates toward the chosen planet and keeps following its orbit — but you can still freely rotate and zoom on top of that.
solarElementRealDataDesc: Each planet opens a card with size, gravity, composition, day and year length, and temperature — real astronomical data, not made up.
solarClickDesc: Click any planet for the camera to close in and open the info card
solarDragDesc: Click and drag to orbit the camera around the scene
solarInfoApproxNote: Approximate values, for illustration purposes only.
solarPlanetSunDayLength: ~27 Earth days (differential rotation, faster at the equator)
solarPlanetSunYearLength: Not applicable — it's the center of the Solar System
solarPlanetSunTemperature: ~5,500°C at the surface, up to ~15,000,000°C at the core
solarPlanetMercuryComposition: Silicate rock and an iron core
solarPlanetMercuryDayLength: 59 Earth days (a full solar day: ~176 Earth days)
solarPlanetMercuryTemperature: -173°C to 427°C (the biggest swing in the system)
solarPlanetMoonComposition: Silicate rock covered by regolith (lunar dust)
solarPlanetMoonDistance: ~384,400 km — the closest celestial body to Earth
solarPlanetMoonDayLength: 27.3 days (tidally locked — always shows the same face)
solarPlanetMoonYearLength: Not applicable — orbits Earth every 27.3 days
solarPlanetJupiterComposition: Hydrogen and helium, in gaseous/metallic state
solarPlanetJupiterTemperature: -145°C at the cloud tops, ~24,000°C at the core (estimated)
solarPlanetSaturnComposition: Hydrogen and helium; ice and rock rings
solarPlanetSaturnTemperature: -189°C at the cloud tops, ~11,700°C at the core (estimated)
solarPlanetUranusTemperature: -224°C to -153°C (the coldest planet in the system)
solarPlanetNeptuneComposition: Water ice, ammonia, and methane (methane gives it the blue color)
journeyIntroDesc: Here, the page scroll is the camera. It travels through a corridor of technologies split into 4 arches: Frontend, Backend, Database, and DevOps. (ScrollControls + useScroll from drei).
journeyScrollDesc: Scroll the page to move forward (or back) through the journey
journeyIntroSubtext: Scroll to move forward. The camera travels through a 3D corridor — no mouse, no dragging.
journeyTechJavaScriptDesc: Solid ES6+ foundation used across every project.
journeyTechTypeScriptDesc: Static typing across the whole fullstack codebase.
journeyTechAngularDesc: Advanced level, used in mission-critical production systems.
journeyTechNextjsDesc: The gap in my own assessment — learned while building this portfolio.
journeyTechViteDesc: Day-to-day bundler on the most recent React projects.
journeyTechNodeDesc: Runtime used for APIs and tooling across the JavaScript ecosystem.
journeyTechJavaDesc: The foundation of my enterprise backend experience.
journeyTechSpringBootDesc: Intermediate-advanced — fullstack architecture in healthcare and government systems.
journeyTechPostgresDesc: Primary relational database in production projects.
journeyTechMySqlDesc: Used in integrations and legacy systems.
journeyTechMongoDesc: For non-relational data and rapid prototyping.
journeyTechSqliteDesc: In smaller projects and local development environments.
journeyTechDockerDesc: Intermediate level — containers in development and deployment.
journeyTechCiCdDesc: Automated build, test, and deploy pipelines.
journeyTechGitLabDesc: Projects with integrated pipelines and code review.
journeyTechGitFlowDesc: Branching workflow for teams and larger-scale projects.
journeyFinalSubtext: Follow my next steps through my socials
